coredata.dc:description: Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) plays a significant role in healthcare, yet the pharmaceutical process generates substantial amounts of organic solid waste (OSW) due to the low proportion of active species in medicinal plants. Traditional disposal methods, such as incineration or landfill, pose significant environmental challenges and fail to recycle valuable resources. Consequently, there is an urgent need for sustainable and high-value utilization strategies to address this issue. Recent research indicates that OSW of TCM is an ideal raw material for preparing biochar-based functional materials due to its high cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin content. Notably, these biochar-based materials exhibit excellent potential in environmental remediation, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and energy storage. However, systematic reviews on the preparation, modification, and application of biochar derived from the OSWs of TCM are lacking. This review summarizes the research progress in these areas and discusses the prospects and challenges associated with biochar-based functional materials from the OSWs of TCM. It aims to provide insights into the high-value preparation and recycling of biochar-based functional materials from TCM waste.

head.ce:title: Advances and prospects of multifunctional biochar-based materials from organic solid waste of traditional Chinese medicine: A review

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) plays a significant role in healthcare, yet the pharmaceutical process generates substantial amounts of organic solid waste (OSW) due to the low proportion of active species in medicinal plants. Traditional disposal methods, such as incineration or landfill, pose significant environmental challenges and fail to recycle valuable resources. Consequently, there is an urgent need for sustainable and high-value utilization strategies to address this issue. Recent research indicates that OSW of TCM is an ideal raw material for preparing biochar-based functional materials due to its high cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin content. Notably, these biochar-based materials exhibit excellent potential in environmental remediation, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and energy storage. However, systematic reviews on the preparation, modification, and application of biochar derived from the OSWs of TCM are lacking. This review summarizes the research progress in these areas and discusses the prospects and challenges associated with biochar-based functional materials from the OSWs of TCM. It aims to provide insights into the high-value preparation and recycling of biochar-based functional materials from TCM waste.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) plays a crucial role in healthcare, with approximately ten million tons consumed annually for disease treatment and prevention. However, the pharmaceutical process generates an estimated 60–70 million tons of organic solid waste (OSW) each year, including discarded roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits of medicinal plants [1,2]. This substantial amount of waste not only occupies land resources but also poses environmental threats by generating greenhouse gases and toxic emissions. Current disposal methods, such as composting, animal feed production, fermentation, and anaerobic digestion, are hindered by inefficiencies, secondary pollution, and low-value usage [3–5]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop novel, efficient, and rational technologies for treating TCM-related OSW.

Biochar, a multifunctional material with a porous structure, large specific surface area, abundant active sites, and good stability [6], has been widely applied in environmental remediation, energy storage, and bioimaging and detection [7–12]. Importantly, biochar can be produced from various raw materials, with OSWs of TCM being a particularly valuable source due to its high cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin content. Consequently, extensive research has focused on the preparation of biochar-based materials from the OSWs of TCM and their application across various fields [13].

Recently, biochar has garnered significant attention as an environmentally friendly, multifunctional material, leading to an exponential increase in related literature. Typically, biochar is produced from agricultural and forestry residues, industrial biomass residues, sewage sludge, livestock and poultry manure, and kitchen waste. Compared to these materials, the OSWs of TCM are rich in lignins and cellulose, have low toxicity, and contain minimal heavy metals. This composition endows biochar with abundant functional groups, a porous structure, and good biocompatibility, making it highly suitable for applications in bioimaging, environmental remediation, and energy storage. However, there is a notable lack of comprehensive reviews on the preparation and application of biochar derived from the OSWs of TCM [14,15]. Converting TCM OSWs into biochar offers an innovative solution for secondary development and resource recycling. Notably, this conversion process can significantly reduce carbon emissions compared to traditional biochar processing methods, aligning with the country's dual goals of carbon neutrality and carbon peaking. Numerous studies have focused on the synthesis, modification, and application of biochar, particularly in soil improvement and water pollution remediation. For instance, Patel [16] investigated the use of biochar to remove antibiotics from wastewater, elucidating the mechanisms involved, while Yang [17] reviewed biochar's role in remediating mercury-contaminated land and explained how it removes mercury from soil. Additionally, significant progress has been made in using biochar for carbon sequestration, emission reduction, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and catalysis. Therefore, a comprehensive and systematic review of biochar is needed.

Unlike previous studies, this review focuses on utilizing cellulose- and lignin-rich OSWs of TCM as a novel raw material for the preparation of multifunctional green biochar. This approach offers a sustainable and innovative solution for managing OSWs of TCM. Additionally, the review comprehensively elaborates on the preparation methods, modification techniques, and application areas of biochar derived from OSWs of TCM. By enhancing the understanding of biochar, this review aims to improve current strategies for managing TCM solid wastes and realize the concept of "turning waste into treasure."

The application of biochar is widely believed to be determined by its physicochemical properties, such as specific surface area, surface functional groups, and porosity. These properties are closely linked to the characteristics of the raw materials. Generally, OSWs with high lignin content yield more biochar after pyrolysis [18]. OSWs rich in volatiles can produce biochar with a large specific surface area and an abundant pore structure [19]. Additionally, the physicochemical properties of biochar are influenced by the preparation technology. Various methods exist for preparing biochar with different physicochemical properties using TCM OSWs as raw materials. This review summarizes several technologies for biochar preparation, including pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave pyrolysis, and gasification (Fig. 1 ).

Pyrolysis is a common method for biochar preparation, involving the thermal decomposition of TCM OSWs under high temperature, pressure, and controlled oxygen conditions [20]. The pyrolysis process generally includes three stages [21]: First, free water is removed from the OSWs at high temperature; second, bound water is eliminated, and lignin and cellulose are depolymerized; finally, biochar is produced through recondensation and polymerization. The structural characteristics and yield of biochar can be adjusted by controlling the pyrolysis temperature, heating rate, pyrolysis time, and gas environment [22]. Research has shown that higher pyrolysis temperatures promote dehydration reactions and gas flow, enhancing the aromaticity, hydrophobicity, surface area, and porosity of biochar by dissociating ester and fatty alkyl groups and reducing oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl groups) [23,24]. Additionally, the heating rate significantly impacts biochar yield by affecting the secondary pyrolysis process of OSWs [25]. Pyrolysis can be categorized into slow pyrolysis and fast pyrolysis based on the heating rate [26]. The characteristics of slow and fast pyrolysis are discussed below.

Slow pyrolysis is a common high-temperature pyrolysis technology characterized by a low heating rate, where reactions typically occur in equilibrium [27,28]. Biochar produced through slow pyrolysis generally has a high yield rate and a stable structure due to the extended vapor residence time during the heating process. Additionally, the surface properties—such as pore structure, surface functional groups, and specific surface area—can be controlled by adjusting the pyrolysis temperature, time, and gas conditions. Slow pyrolysis has been widely used in various fields, including soil improvement, environmental remediation, carbon fixation, and aerobic composting [29]. For instance, Dai et al. prepared biochar from TCM residues at different temperatures for tetracycline adsorption, finding that higher temperatures resulted in a larger specific surface area, greater porosity, and larger pore diameters, thereby enhancing adsorption capacity for tetracycline [30]. However, the relationship between pyrolysis parameters and temperature is not always linear, as factors like heating rate, residence time, and biomass fineness also significantly affect biochar properties [31]. Furthermore, the practical application of slow pyrolysis is limited by its long duration and high energy consumption.

Rapid pyrolysis is another technology for biochar preparation, characterized by rapidly increasing the temperature at a heating rate of ≥200 °C/min under anaerobic and atmospheric conditions [32]. This process produces a mixture of condensable liquids, gases, and char due to the absence of oxygen or air [33,34]. Compared to slow pyrolysis, rapid pyrolysis differs in several ways: (1) higher heating rates; (2) isothermal reactions at the terminal temperature; (3) shorter pyrolysis time, and (4) differences in the products. Typically, slow pyrolysis primarily produces biochar [35], while rapid pyrolysis mainly yields bio-oil products [36,37]. However, there have been numerous studies on biochar derived from rapid pyrolysis. For example, Liu et al. prepared MgO-biochar from lotus seed shells through rapid pyrolysis for phosphorus adsorption. The results showed that MgO-biochar plays a crucial role in removing phosphorus due to its porous structure and large specific surface area [38].

Microwave-assisted pyrolysis is an emerging technology known for its low energy consumption, uniform heating, fast heating rate, and high energy efficiency [39–41]. It is recognized as a promising alternative to conventional pyrolysis, simplifying operations while enhancing product yield and quality [42]. Due to these advantages, microwave-assisted pyrolysis is widely used in the preparation of biochar and biofuels [43]. Biochar produced through this method exhibits excellent pore properties, with more pores and a larger specific surface area. This is attributed to the uniform release of volatile matter throughout the OSW particles and the minimal presence of pyrolysis carbon particles in the char's micropores [44].

Hydrothermal carbonization is a promising technology offering various advantages over conventional heating methods, such as a simple process, low operating temperature, and high conversion rate [45]. Notably, hydrothermal carbonization can directly convert wet biomass into biochar, avoiding the tedious drying process, reducing energy loss, and improving the yield and quality of biochar. This technology is widely used for preparing biochar from wet materials [46]. Research has shown that biochar produced by hydrothermal carbonization possesses abundant oxygen-containing functional groups due to hydrolysis and hydration functions under high self-pressure [47]. However, the specific surface area and pore volumes of biochar produced by hydrothermal carbonization are lower, attributed to the low operating temperatures and incomplete pyrolysis of the biomass [48].

Additionally, gasification is considered a viable technology for biochar preparation due to its short operation time, environmental friendliness, and high efficiency [49]. Biochar produced by gasification features a highly microporous structure and large specific surface area, attributed to higher carbonization temperatures and syngas yield, enhancing its application potential [50,51]. However, the primary purpose of gasification is to maximize energy recovery by producing high-yield syngas, resulting in lower biochar yield [52].

Acid-modified biochar is produced by treating biomass materials with acidic reagents, either before or after pyrolysis, using activators such as H2SO4, HNO3, and H3PO4 [56]. This method is widely employed to enhance specific surface area and increase microporous structure, as acids effectively remove impurities from the biochar surface. Additionally, acid modification introduces more oxygen-containing functional groups, improving pollutant adsorption [57]. However, acid treatment reduces alkali metal elements on the biochar surface, inhibiting complexation with pollutants and weakening adsorption capacity, especially for inorganic pollutants like PO4 3− [58]. Therefore, selecting appropriate modification methods based on pollutant types and characteristics is crucial. Notably, different acids and concentrations can variably impact biochar's surface properties. For instance, HCl increases the specific surface area of biochar, while HNO3 decreases it, due to varying corrosive abilities [59]. Hence, the choice of acid and its concentration should be carefully considered to maximize specific surface area and active functional groups.

Alkali-modified biochar, typically prepared by treating biomass materials with alkali reagents before pyrolysis, commonly uses activators like KOH and NaOH. This modification significantly enhances biochar's pore structure, specific surface area, and surface oxygen-containing functional groups [60]. Additionally, alkali-modified biochar exhibits stronger hydrophobicity and higher cation exchange capacity, enhancing its adsorption affinity for pollutants [61,62]. KOH activation, in particular, is notable for creating biochar with excellent microporous, mesoporous, or combined microporous/mesoporous structures.

Generally, the surface properties of acid- and alkali-modified biochar are significantly improved, featuring a larger specific surface area, more oxygen-containing functional groups, and a richer pore structure. However, certain drawbacks limit the utility of these modification methods, such as the difficulty of recycling chemical reagents and the environmental damage caused by strong acids and bases [63]. Therefore, when using acid- and alkali-modified biochar, researchers should aim to enhance biochar performance while also focusing on the recycling of chemical reagents and mitigating the potential environmental impacts of these modifications.

Magnetically modified biochar is a composite material created through pyrolytic activation or chemical co-precipitation of biochar and magnetic media (Fe or FexOy), exhibiting excellent adsorption properties for organic pollutants, inorganic pollutants, and heavy metals. This material allows for solid-liquid separation using an external magnetic field [64]. Magnetic modification has significantly enhanced biochar's role in facilitating solid-liquid separation and reducing the risk of secondary pollutant release [65]. Additionally, the formation of new active adsorption sites has notably improved pollutant adsorption performance. However, the coverage of biochar pores by magnetic media reduces specific surface area and porosity, partially inhibiting pollutant adsorption [66]. Moreover, the application of magnetically modified biochar is limited by secondary pollution from Fe particle leaching and poor cycle stability due to magnetic loss [67]. Therefore, when employing magnetization technology to modify biochar, it is crucial to consider the characteristics of the pollutants, optimize the preparation process to enhance the bonding force between magnetic particles and biochar, and use appropriate magnetic regeneration agents to produce green biochar with high adsorption performance and recyclability.

Htero-element doping is considered a promising method for modifying biochar due to its ability to introduce more abundant surface functional groups, more adsorption active sites, and better stability. Currently, nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) are ideal hetero-elements for biochar doping [68,69]. Among these, nitrogen doping alters the surface charge and surface pH of biochar, enhancing the adsorption of acid gases (carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide), heavy metals, and organic pollutants. Additionally, the introduction of functional groups such as pyridine-N, pyrrorole-N, graphite-N, and oxide-N gives biochar the potential to catalyze redox reactions and activate persulfate [70,71]. Oxygen doping increases the number of oxygen-containing functional groups (-OH, –COOH) on the biochar surface, changes its electron donation ability, and increases specific surface area and pore size, thereby improving the adsorption of inorganic pollutants and heavy metal ions [72]. However, the increased polarity from oxygen-containing functional groups can hinder the adsorption of hydrophobic organic compounds [73]. Moreover, doping with sulfur and phosphorus significantly enhances the specific surface area and surface-active functional groups of biochar, boosting its adsorption capacity and catalytic efficiency [74,75]. Despite these benefits, the method's drawbacks, such as low doping efficiency and the environmental impact of doping reagents, severely limit its large-scale application.

Additionally, UV irradiation has been used to increase the specific surface area and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of biochar [76]. Ball milling, another modification technique, enhances the surface properties of biochar by reducing particle size, thereby increasing its specific surface area. This method also exposes more oxygen-containing functional groups on the biochar surface, which boosts its adsorption capacity [77]. Anaerobic digestion modification is employed to increase the surface area, oxygenated functional groups, and surface mineral composition of biochar [78].

Presently, biochar has been widely used in soil improvement, water purification, energy storage, catalysis, CQDs detection, carbon sequestration and emission reduction, and aerobic composting (Fig. 3 ) due to its unique structure, low cost, large storage capacity, and environmental friendliness.

Numerous studies have confirmed the ability of biochar to restore soil due to several reasons: (1) it softens soil [90–92], (2) reduces soil water loss [93], (3) improves soil nutrient content [94–96], (4) alkalizes the soil, and 5) enables in-situ immobilization of heavy metal ions and organic pollutants, aiding in soil contamination remediation [97,98]. Nowadays, extensive scholars have applied the biochar prepared from OSWs of TCM to improve the soil environment. For example, Ma [99] applied OSWs of TCM to soil remediation. The results showed that the OSWs of TCM could not only improve soil cation exchange capacity and reduce soil heavy metal content, but also alleviate soil acidification to a certain extent. Wang [100] prepared biochar for soil remediation. The results demonstrated that the adding of biochar to soil could effectively remediate toxic metal-contaminated soils due to its large specific surface area and abundant cation exchange capacity. Although biochar has significant advantages in the field of soil improvement, its disadvantages cannot be ignored. For example, toxic substances such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, chlorinated hydrocarbons, and dioxins will be released during biochar preparation, which will cause serious damage to soil. Additionally, toxic elements such as heavy metals fixed through biochar have the risk of secondary release, posing a potential threat to the environment and ecology [101].

The use of OSWs of TCM as a bulking agent has been demonstrated to effectively improve the composting process, increase efficiency, and accelerate compost maturation. Additionally, fertilizers derived from aerobic composting with TCM OSWs are considered greener and more effective, largely due to the low heavy metal content of biochar from OSWs and the immobilization effect of biochar's oxygen-containing functional groups, which reduce heavy metal enrichment. These fertilizers significantly benefit the planting industry [102]. Researchers have identified several mechanisms by which biochar accelerates compost maturation: 1) enhancing the degradation rate and final degradation degree of organic matter by reducing nitrogen loss and greenhouse gas emissions during composting [103,104], and 2) providing ample habitats for microorganisms due to the porous nature and large surface area of biochar from OSWs, which accelerates the compost maturation process, alters microbial community structure, and reduces pathogenic ARGs in mature compost [105,106]. Liu et al. [107] found that bamboo biochar promotes the maturation of food waste compost by improving microorganism growth during the thermophilic stage and increasing the degradation rate of organic matter, thereby speeding up fertilizer maturation. Moreover, using OSWs in aerobic composting promotes the recycling of TCM OSWs.

Biochar is considered a highly promising material for wastewater purification due to its rich surface active groups, large specific surface area, and extensive pore structure [108,109]. This makes it effective in treating various types of wastewater, including organic matter, heavy metal ions, and nitrogen and phosphorus sewage [110].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of organic matter from sewage
Removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater
Removal of nitrogen and phosphorus from wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The enrichment of inorganic phosphorus and nitrogen is a primary driver of water eutrophication [139]. Inorganic nitrogen typically exists as NH4 +-N and NO3 −, while inorganic phosphorus mainly exists as PO4 3−. If not properly managed, the accumulation of these compounds in water can lead to rapid plankton reproduction, severely harming aquatic ecosystems. Thus, exploring green, cost-effective, and efficient methods for removing nitrogen and phosphorus from sewage has become essential. Among the various approaches, adsorbent adsorption is considered effective for this purpose [140,141]. Biochar, with its large specific surface area, porous structure, abundant surface-active functional groups, and high cation exchange capacity, is regarded as a promising high-efficiency adsorbent for nitrogen and phosphorus compounds [142]. Biochar can remove N and P compounds from sewage through chemisorption (ion exchange, surface complexation), physical adsorption (pore filling, electrostatic attraction), and chemical precipitation (co-precipitation with surface alkali metals) [143,144] (Fig. 6 ). For example, Zhou [145] demonstrated that biochar prepared by bamboo pyrolysis effectively removes NH4 + due to its rich surface ionizable groups. Additionally, the NO3 − adsorption strength of date biochar prepared at high temperatures is significantly higher than that of biochar produced at lower temperatures [146]. This increased efficiency is attributed to the reduction of oxygen-containing functional groups at high pyrolysis temperatures, which weakens the negative charge repulsion of NO3 − ions. High-temperature pyrolysis also results in a larger specific surface area and porosity, enhancing NO3 − adsorption.

Furthermore, modifications to biochar can enhance the removal of PO4 3− and NO3 − due to improved performance. For instance, Wang [147] developed magnetic Mg-loaded biochar for the immobilization of PO4 3− and NH4 +, significantly enhancing the biochar's ability to immobilize these ions and achieving the goal of recycling. Additionally, phosphorus-rich biochar can be used as a soil conditioner to promote crop growth [148]. Thus, recovering biochar that has adsorbed nitrogen and phosphorus and applying it to soil can help promote the nitrogen and phosphorus cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emerging application of biochar-based materials for energy storage and conversion
ce:section.ce:para: Energy shortages have become a significant challenge for humanity due to the rapid consumption and limited storage of fossil fuels [149]. Compared to traditional batteries, biochar supercapacitors offer high efficiency, environmental sustainability, and long cycle lifespans, making them a desirable electrode material for addressing energy shortages [150–152]. The heteroatomic groups on the surface of biochar effectively increase the material's wettability and reduce ion transfer resistance, enhancing the supercapacitor's energy density and improving energy storage. However, biochar prepared directly from the OSWs of TCM typically lacks a large surface area and well-developed pores, resulting in poor charge-discharge performance for supercapacitor electrodes. To achieve high capacitance, biochar must be modified to increase its specific surface area and pore structure, enabling high power charging, discharging, and high reversible capacity. For instance, Gopalakrishnan [153] prepared N, S-codoping worm-like hierarchical porous carbon using ginger as a precursor. The optimized activated carbon (DAGC) exhibited abundant porous structures, facilitating the effective diffusion of electrolyte ions, enhancing surface wettability, and reducing electrolyte ion transport resistance, thereby achieving rapid ion transfer. Similarly, Zhou [154] successfully prepared nanoflower-like layered porous carbon from licorice residue, demonstrating excellent capacitance performance and good electrochemical stability. In addition to energy storage applications, biochar can be used as a fuel, promoting sustainable energy use and reducing energy consumption. However, biochar's insufficient combustion strength and the production of dust and polluting gases during combustion limit its application as a fuel. Thus, converting biochar into high calorific value, clean, and environmentally friendly formed fuel is crucial for alleviating energy and environmental pressures.

It has been shown that carbon-rich materials such as carbon black, carbon nanotubes, and graphene have been widely used in the field of catalysis. Similarly, biochar also has been applied in the catalysis field for environment remediation, energy transformation and compound synthesis due to the large specific surface area, excellent conductivity and light absorption, and environmentally friendly [155,156].

Biochar is a potential high-efficiency catalytic material and carrier for catalysts in environmental remediation. As a catalyst, biochar's environmentally persistent free radicals (EPFRs) can react with O2 to form reactive oxygen species (ROS), including superoxide radicals (.O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (.OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). These ROS, with high redox abilities, can oxidize or reduce pollutants [157–160]. For instance, Guo's group prepared BC/Fe(III)/PS for sulfamethoxazole disposal, demonstrating that the EPFRs in biochar act as electron donors, facilitating electron transfer between BC and Fe(III). This process promotes Fe(II) generation, which activates PS to produce .OH and .SO4 2− radicals, effectively degrading sulfamethoxazole in sewage [161]. Similarly, Xu et al. prepared peanut shell biochar for Cr(VI) removal, showing that biochar serves as both an electron donor and shuttle, aiding in the reduction of Cr(VI) during the sorption process [162]. As a catalyst carrier, biochar enhances adsorption ability, light utilization rate, and the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers due to its large surface area, black nature, and good conductivity. For example, Shi et al. synthesized wild jujube shell porous biochar (pBC) and constructed the pBC/Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br2/g-C3N4/C heterojunctions for ibuprofen and norfloxacin degradation. The results showed that biochar improved adsorption ability, light utilization rate, and charge separation, leading to enhanced catalytic oxidation activity [163,164].

The specific surface area and catalytic centers of a catalyst are crucial for the synthesis of organic compounds. Catalysts with larger specific surface areas and abundant active centers enhance yield and reaction rates in organic synthesis. Biochar has been identified as an excellent catalyst carrier for designing and preparing solid acid catalysts due to its porous structure, large specific surface area, abundant active sites, enriched functional groups, and thermal stability [165]. For example, Ameta's group developed a biochar/Fe3O4@SiO2–Ag catalyst for synthesizing benzyl pyrazolyl coumarin derivatives. Their findings indicate that biochar significantly improves the yield of these derivatives by providing more active sites through its extensive surface area and enriched functional groups [166]. Similarly, Krishnan et al. discovered that acid-functionalized hydrochar, derived from orange peel, supplies more acidic functional groups and serves as an effective catalyst for synthesizing compounds like 5,5'-(propane-2,2-diyl)bis(2-methylfuran) and 5,5'-(thiophen-2-ylmethylene)bis(2-methylfuran) [167].

Hydrogen (H2), with its high calorific value and clean combustion products, is a promising alternative energy source amid the excessive depletion of fossil fuels. Various methods for H2 production include fermentation, methane cracking, and water electrolysis [168]. However, these methods often suffer from low H2 yield and high energy consumption, limiting their practical application. Adding catalysts has been shown to effectively address these limitations. Among various catalysts, biochar stands out due to its large specific surface area, porous structure, and abundance of active groups [169]. For example, Sunyoto's group used biochar to enhance hydrogen production from the anaerobic digestion of food waste. The results indicated that biochar significantly improved the microbial environment and accelerated microbial metabolism, thus increasing H2 generation rate and yield [170]. Similarly, Patel's group employed biochar for H2 production via methane cracking. Their findings showed that biochar's large specific surface area, rich porosity, and acidic active groups played a crucial role in boosting H2 production efficiency [171].

Quantum dots (QDs) is a type of semiconductor materials with small size (diameter <10 nm), which is known for its unique photoelectrochemical properties, and has been widely applied in the fields of energy, bioimaging, and molecular detection [172,173]. Hitherto, various of QDs (i.e Ag2Se, CdSe, CuInS2, graphene, and CQDs) have been explored and attracted numerous attention [174–176]. Among them, CQDs is a novel material with the multiple advantages of high biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of synthesis, and facile modulation, and has been widely applied in the fields of bioimaging, energy transformation, and molecular detection [177,178]. Currently, two methods of top-down (decomposition of macromolecules) and bottom-up (polymerization of tiny molecules) were used to synthesized the CQDs [179]. Besides, to extend the applications of CQDs, several novel techniques (e.g. arc discharge, laser ablation, acid oxidation, and microwave pyrolysis) are adopted to adjust the properties of size, composition, water solubility, fluorescence properties of CQDs [180–186]. Next, The latest application of CQDs synthesized from the OSWs of TCM were summarized in our review.

It is reported that the metal ion can directly or indirectly quench the fluorescence of CQDs through various pathway. Therefore, CQDs were widely applied in metal ion detection [187]. For example, Ramezani [188] et al. have successfully prepared the CQDs with the quince powder as the raw material, and the CQDs achieve the high-efficient detection for As3+. Sun et al. [189] synthesized CQDs using Lycium barbarum as a precursor for Fe3+ detection, the result show that the surface hydroxyl groups of CQDs could complex with Fe3+, which promote electron transfer and non-radiative electron/hole recombination, then significantly quenching fluorescence and realize the detection of Fe3+. Additionally, CQDs can also be used to detect organic compounds [190–193]. For instance, Ensafi [190] et al. have disclosed that the CQDs with the safflor as the raw material can be used to detect the procainamide, and the mechanism can be attributed to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds were formed between the procainamide and CQDs, which accelerated electron transfer and led to fluorescence quench.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in bioimaging due to their good biocompatibility, excellent optical property. Besides, the small size of CQDs enables their can label the perinuclear cytoplasm through the endocytosis of cell (Fig. 7 ) [194]. To date, CQDs have been applied in bioimaging for monitoring the growth of different cancer cells, and the reason can be attributed to the transferrin on the surface of CQDs can efficiently bind to antigens of cancer cells. Besides, the CQDs can also realize the selective labeling of other cells (eg. Lymphocytes, stem cells etc.) through binding of antigen-antibody [195]. Notably, The CQDs can be used as optical imaging agents for plant cells and microorganisms (bacteria, fungi). For example, N-CQDs of Panax notoginseng can be effectively used for imaging of bacteria, fungi and plant cells due to the N-CQDs can specifically recognize cell membranes [196].

Recently, CQDs have emerged as promising nanomaterials for drug delivery in cancer treatment, owing to their tunable surface groups, small size, and excellent biocompatibility. Compared to other drug carriers, CQDs offer several advantages: 1) achieving both intracellular and extracellular therapeutic effects, 2) efficient drug delivery, 3) minimal adverse effects, 4) high drug loading capacity, and 5) controlled release [197–199]. Numerous studies have demonstrated the efficacy of CQDs as drug carriers for disease therapy. For instance, Hu et al. developed multifunctional nanoprobes (RCD-Pt(iv)/PEG–CS–DA) capable of efficiently labeling and killing cancer cells under acidic conditions [200]. Similarly, Tejwan's group synthesized CQDs from ginseng for targeted delivery of rutin in breast cancer therapy, showing excellent anticancer effects both in vivo and in vitro [201]. Beyond conventional anticancer drugs (such as cisplatin and methotrexate), combining CQDs with photodynamic therapy (PDT) offers another effective cancer treatment approach. This combination generates ROS under fluorescence excitation to kill cancer cells, while simultaneously enabling fluorescence imaging to monitor cancer cell growth during treatment [202]. Additionally, CQDs have shown potential as carriers for selective antibacterial and anti-inflammatory treatments. For example, John et al. prepared photoluminescent CQDs from wheat bran using a hydrothermal method and coupled them with amoxicillin (AMX) for antibacterial use in vivo. The CQDs demonstrated significant drug-carrying capacity (up to 69.8 % of antibacterial drugs) and exhibited pH-sensitive release, rapidly treating bacterial infections under acidic conditions [203]. Therefore, biochar-derived quantum dots hold great potential as carriers of antimicrobial drugs.

Excessive CO2 emissions have caused dramatic climate changes, posing serious threats to ecosystems [204]. Developing efficient technologies for carbon capture, sequestration, and storage is crucial. Biochar, known for its capability to significantly adsorb CO2 through intermolecular hydrogen bonds or coordination forces between its surface functional groups and CO2, emerges as an ideal material for carbon storage and sequestration [205–207]. Zhang's synthesis of AC-KOH-N, using acacia as the raw precursor, demonstrated that nitrogen doping increases the specific surface area and microporous structure of biochar, thereby enhancing CO2 adsorption capacity by 2.7 % [208]. Incorporating biochar into cement-based materials effectively reduces CO2 emissions due to its porous structure and abundant active groups [209]. Gupta's research indicated that adding biochar to concrete materials enhances their CO2 fixation capacity by 2.5%–3% [210]. Moreover, Yu's study highlighted that biochar derived from the OSWs of TCM not only reduces CO2 emissions but also promotes soil carbon sequestration [211]. Converting OSWs of TCM into biochar represents an efficient, valuable, and innovative method for CO2 reduction, offering significant environmental benefits.

Prospects: TCM plays a predominant role in healthcare. However, the pharmaceutical process of TCM generates millions of tons of OSWs annually, posing a significant disposal challenge. Conventional uses for OSWs of TCM include the preparation of organic fertilizers, cultivation of edible mushrooms, feed additives, fuel production, and papermaking. However, these applications are often limited by low value, secondary pollution, and tedious operations. In contrast, biochar-based functional materials derived from OSWs of TCM present an emerging and promising application in areas such as soil improvement, sewage purification, catalytic redox reactions, energy storage and transformation, biological imaging, drug delivery, and fluorescence detection. Thus, the preparation of biochar-based functional materials offers a high-value utilization method for OSWs of TCM, providing a new perspective for the recycling and efficient use of these wastes.

Challenges: although the preparation of biochar-based functional materials presents a promising and high-value utilization method for OSWs of TCM, several challenges limit the large-scale application of this emerging disposal strategy.
(1)
The yield and properties of biochar are influenced by both the pyrolysis process and the characteristics of the raw materials. Due to the diverse sources, complex compositions, and significant variations in the physical and chemical characteristics of TCM, the yield, property, and structure of biochar can vary considerably. Additionally, biochar yield and properties can differ even when using the same OSWs of TCM but under different pyrolysis processes. Therefore, selecting specific reaction conditions and parameters based on the characteristics of the raw materials is crucial to achieving optimal biochar performance and yield.
(2)
For biochar to be effective in environmental remediation, surface properties such as specific surface area, pore parameters, and surface functional groups are critical. Single preparation methods, including pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, and microwave-assisted pyrolysis, often fall short of practical requirements. Thus, developing innovative techniques for preparing and modifying biochar with superior surface properties remains essential.
(3)
For the emerging application of drug delivery, essential factors include biocompatibility, high-efficiency drug delivery, high drug loading capacity, and controlled release. Biochar obtained from conventional preparation techniques for CQDs often fails to meet these demands. Consequently, extensive efforts are needed to develop suitable, universal, and mature techniques that fulfill these requirements.
(4)
For fluorescence detection using CQDs, fluorescence efficiency and lifetime are crucial parameters. However, like other biochar-based materials, single preparation methods often fail to achieve the desired fluorescence efficiency and lifetime for target molecule detection. Therefore, exploring new, universal, and mature techniques for CQDs preparation is essential.
(5)
Reports on the industrial utilization of biochar-based functional materials derived from the OSWs of TCM in soil amendments, wastewater purification, catalysts, CO2 fixation, supercapacitor electrodes, and aerobic composting are limited. These applications remain largely experimental. Significant efforts in engineering theory and technique are required to advance their industrial utilization.

Addressing the pressing issue of OSW accumulation in the pharmaceutical process of TCM, the preparation of multifunctional biochar-based materials from OSWs of TCM has emerged as an effective strategy. Biochar-based materials, known for their low toxicity, good stability, abundant pore structure, and large specific surface area, are widely applied in soil amendments, wastewater purification, catalysts, CO2 fixation, supercapacitor electrodes, and aerobic composting. This review comprehensively summarizes the preparation methods, modification techniques, and applications of biochar from OSWs of TCM, including emerging applications in catalysis, bioimaging, and fluorescence detection. Additionally, the prospects and challenges of converting OSWs of TCM into multifunctional biochar-based materials are discussed, offering a new perspective on the recycling of TCM resources.

Chao Chen: Writing – original draft. Luqi Yang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Xiaofei Zhang: Resources, Methodology. Chongbo Zhao: Methodology, Investigation.
